topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a stark, undeniable truth. My piece, the one I poured weeks of late nights into, was live. But the byline belonged to a complete stranger. A wave of cold washed over me, starting in my chest and spreading outward until my hands began to tremble. I reread the article, the words that were undeniably mine, now draped in another person’s reputation. A heavy, suffocating silence settled in the small apartment. I stared at the screen, the glow illuminating the deep lines of exhaustion etched around my eyes. Every breath felt shallow, a physical manifestation of the error. I slowly closed the laptop, the soft click sounding deafening in the quiet room. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of deflation, as if the very ground beneath me had shifted.

[story 2]

When I saw the attribution, the world tilted. It was a sickening lurch in my stomach, a sudden drop in temperature that made my skin prickle. I leaned back in my chair, gripping the arms until my knuckles turned white. How could this happen? I had double-checked every detail, yet here it was—my voice, my argument, belonging to someone else entirely. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I started pacing the cramped office, the worn carpet muffling the sound of my hurried steps. Each footfall seemed to echo the internal chaos. I stopped by the filing cabinet, running a hand over the cold metal, my gaze fixed on the surface as if searching for an answer that simply wasn't there. The realization settled in, a lead weight pressing down on my shoulders, demanding acknowledgment.

[story 3]

The email arrived at midnight, stark and unforgiving. It confirmed my worst fear: the publication had mistakenly attributed my work to an author with a strikingly similar name. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, an instinctual urge to delete, to erase the evidence, but I couldn't bring myself to touch the screen. A slow, agonizing burn began in my chest, a dull ache that radiated outwards. I sank onto the edge of my bed, pulling my knees to my chest. The air felt thick and hard to move through. I kept replaying the submission process in my head, searching desperately for the lapse, the moment where my identity had been so carelessly overwritten. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot path down my cheek, quickly followed by a desperate attempt to blink it away. The sheer unfairness of it all left me feeling hollowed out.
